mission.title: Install and Configure MongoDB Community Operator
mission.description: The MongoDB Community Operator allows you to deploy and manage MongoDB Community instances on Kubernetes. It simplifies the deployment process and provides lifecycle management for MongoDB resources. However, note that this operator is deprecated, and users are encouraged to migrate to the new repository for continued support.

steps.title: Add the MongoDB Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the MongoDB Community Operator Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add mongodb https://mongodb.github.io/mongodb-kubernetes-operator

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the official MongoDB Helm repository.
> Updates your local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the MongoDB Community Operator
steps.description:
This step installs the MongoDB Community Operator using Helm, specifying the namespace and version.

```bash

kubectl create namespace mongodb

helm install mongodb-community-operator mongodb/mongodb-kubernetes-operator --namespace mongodb --version 0.13.0

```

> Creates a dedicated namespace for MongoDB resources.
> Installs the MongoDB Community Operator in the mongodb namespace.

steps.title: Verify the Operator installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the MongoDB Community Operator is running correctly in the cluster.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n mongodb

```

> Lists all pods in the mongodb namespace to verify the operator is running.

steps.title: Create a MongoDB resource
steps.description:
This step creates a MongoDB resource to deploy an instance of MongoDB.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mongodb.com/v1
kind: MongoDB
metadata:
  name: my-mongo
  namespace: mongodb
spec:
  members: 3
  type: ReplicaSet
  version: 4.4.6
  podSpec:
    resources:
      limits:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "1Gi"
      requests:
        cpu: "250m"
        memory: "512Mi"
EOF

```

> Creates a MongoDB ReplicaSet with 3 members.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mongodb.com/v1
kind: MongoDB
metadata:
  name: my-mongo
  namespace: mongodb
spec:
  members: 3

  podSpec:
    resources:
      limits:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "1Gi"
      requests:
        cpu: "250m"
        memory: "512Mi"
EOF

steps.title: Verify the MongoDB resource
steps.description:
This step checks if the MongoDB instance is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get mongodb -n mongodb

```

> Lists all MongoDB resources in the mongodb namespace.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the MongoDB Community Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the MongoDB Community Operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all MongoDB instances and data associated with them.

```bash

helm uninstall mongodb-community-operator --namespace mongodb

kubectl delete namespace mongodb

```

> Uninstalls the MongoDB Community Operator.
> Deletes the mongodb namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the MongoDB Community Operator
upgrade.description:
Backup your MongoDB data, upgrade the operator, and verify the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl cp mongodb/my-mongo-0:/data/db /tmp/mongo-backup

helm upgrade mongodb-community-operator mongodb/mongodb-kubernetes-operator --namespace mongodb --version 0.13.0

kubectl get pods -n mongodb

```

> Backup MongoDB data before upgrading.
> Upgrades the MongoDB Community Operator.
> Verifies that all pods are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create MongoDB resource'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to insufficient permissions or incorrect resource specifications.

**Fix:**
Ensure that your Kubernetes service account has the necessary permissions and that the resource specifications are correct.

troubleshooting.title: Pod is in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may happen if the MongoDB instance fails to start due to configuration issues.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs using 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n mongodb' to identify the issue.

troubleshooting.title: MongoDB instance not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Network policies or service misconfigurations may prevent access.

**Fix:**
Verify the service configuration and ensure that network policies allow traffic to the MongoDB pods.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient resources'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the cluster does not have enough resources to schedule the MongoDB pods.

**Fix:**
Check the resource requests and limits, and ensure your cluster has sufficient resources available.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for MongoDB 4.4.6, improved error handling during resource creation.
versionNotes.deprecations: No specific deprecations in this version.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v0.11, ensure to review the migration guide for any breaking changes.

resolution.summary: You should see the MongoDB instance running with the command 'kubectl get mongodb -n mongodb' showing the status as 'Ready'.

kind: MongoDB

EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary tools installed.
